title: One-Shot Bodies, Streaming, Uploads, Decompression, and Resource Limits · Browser and Node Client Systems · Narrowing Workshop
summary: An accessibility product team integrating a staging browser API must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for one-shot bodies, streaming, uploads, decompression, and resource limits, producing bounded streaming body pipeline. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch bounded streaming body pipeline while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, ch…
objectives: Treat request and response bodies as owned one-shot streams with explicit read, close, replay, and cleanup rules. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Limit compressed and decoded response reads before allocation, parsing, logging, or persistence. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Stream large uploads with known ownership, backpressure, progress, cancellation, and retryability. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop One Shot Body Ownership
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK streams a 20 MiB upload and receives a 40 KiB compressed body that expands past 50 MiB. Define one-shot ownership, replay, compressed and decoded limits, cancellation, trailers, and cleanup.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop One Shot Body Ownership.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for treat request and response bodies as owned one-shot streams with explicit read, close, replay, and cleanup rules. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop One Shot Body Ownership, keep One Shot Body Ownership connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Defend against decompression bombs, oversized structured values, deep nesting, and unbounded field counts. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
